title: Honor, Moral Courage & Bearing Cost for Principle

supplementary.title: [For Student] Case studies in corporate whistleblowing

competence_markers.description: They have written a personal honor code that is specific, honest, and non-aspirational — it describes how they actually live, tested against real behavior
They demonstrate moral courage in appropriate contexts — speaking up when they observe wrongdoing, raising concerns with authority, and accepting the social cost of truth-telling
They practice leadership accountability — owning group outcomes, absorbing blame upward, distributing credit downward — in real leadership contexts
They can analyze complex moral scenarios with nuance, identifying competing values, weighing costs, and arriving at reasoned positions without false certainty or paralyzed indecision
They understand and can articulate the distinction between whistleblowing and cowardice, between moral courage and self-righteousness, between loyalty and complicity
Their reputation capital is visible — people who know them describe them as trustworthy, reliable, and honest under pressure
They conduct regular integrity audits and respond to the findings with genuine behavioral change, not with guilt or defensiveness

Their commitment-keeping from Weathering Storms has become automatic — so reliable that it no longer requires tracking for most commitments, though they maintain the practice for high-stakes obligations

narration_prompts.detail: Not the document — the principle. What are the things you will not do, regardless of cost? How did you arrive at those lines? Have they been tested?

narration_prompts.prompt: Describe a situation where integrity required courage.
narration_prompts.detail: Either from your own life or from your studies. What was the cost of the right action? What was the cost of silence? Which would you choose, and why?
How is it different from individual accountability? Give an example of a leader who practiced it, and an example of a leader who did not. What were the consequences?
Where is the line? How do you decide when staying silent is no longer an option? What frameworks do you use?
What have you invested in it? What would the people who know you best say about your integrity — honestly, not generously?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you failed to live up to your own standard.
narration_prompts.detail: Not someone else's standard — yours. What happened? What did you learn? What did you do about it?

cross_connections.description: IC1.3-C is the moral backbone of 4.3. Leadership without accountability is authority without legitimacy. Every principle in this packet — honor codes, leadership accountability, moral courage, bearing blame — directly prepares for the leadership formation of Domain 4.3. No student advances significantly in 4.3 without demonstrated IC1.3-C maturity.

Civic life demands moral courage — the willingness to speak truth to institutional power, to hold elected officials accountable, to resist unjust law, and to serve the common good at personal cost. IC1.3-C provides the character formation; 4.5 provides the arena.

In business, integrity is a long-term strategic asset. The entrepreneur who cuts corners gains short-term advantage and loses long-term trust. The entrepreneur who builds on reputation capital — delivering what they promise, owning their failures, treating customers and partners with integrity — builds something that compounds over decades. IC1.3-C's framework of reputation capital applies directly.

The craftsperson's honor code is the quality of their work. The joint that holds when no one is watching, the weld that is strong where it cannot be seen, the measurement that is precise because the craftsperson's standard demands it — these are integrity in physical form. The craftsperson who cuts corners is lying with their hands. IC1.3-C gives this its moral framework.

The integrity audit of IC1.3-C depends on the self-examination capacity of IC1.6-C. You cannot evaluate your own integrity without the interior honesty that IC1.6 develops. The two sub-domains are deeply intertwined at Standing Ground.

The ethics of whistleblowing, the legitimacy of civil disobedience, the moral obligations of citizens and leaders — these are IC1.3-C applied to political life. The student who has wrestled with moral courage in personal contexts is prepared to reason about it in civic contexts.

content.why_this_matters:
You learned to tell the truth. You learned to keep your word and own your impact in groups. Those habits were hard to build and they are genuinely valuable. But they were built in relatively safe environments — your family, your teams, your small communities. The consequences of honesty were manageable. The cost of accountability was proportionate.

Standing Ground removes those protections. The questions now are harder:

What do you do when telling the truth costs you something real — a friendship, a position, a reputation with people whose approval you want? What do you do when the group you belong to is doing something wrong, and speaking up means standing alone? What do you do when you discover that someone you respect or depend on is acting without integrity, and silence would be easier, and safer, and no one would blame you for it?

These are not hypothetical questions. They are the questions that define the moral lives of adults. And most adults fail them — not because they are bad people, but because no one prepared them for the moment when integrity requires courage, when accountability demands risk, and when the right thing to do is obvious but terrifying.

This packet prepares you for those moments. It introduces honor — the concept that there are things a person of integrity simply does not do, regardless of benefit or consequence. It introduces moral courage — the capacity to act on principle when the cost is real and the support is absent. It introduces leadership accountability — the understanding that those with authority absorb blame upward and direct credit downward, and that excuses flow down in systems that fail. And it introduces the most difficult question in the ethics of integrity: when does loyalty become complicity, and when does staying silent make you responsible for what you allowed to continue?

You will not master these things by reading about them. You will master them by facing them — in case studies first, in real life eventually. This packet gives you the framework. Life gives you the test.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Honor as a code** — the understanding that there are things a person of integrity simply does not do, regardless of benefit, consequence, or whether anyone would ever find out
2. **Reputation capital** — the long-term strategic value of being the kind of person others trust with consequential things; integrity as compound investment, not constraint
3. **Bearing blame for others** — the leadership application of accountability: absorbing the cost of collective failure publicly while addressing the causes privately
4. **Leadership accountability** — the principle that authority and accountability are inseparable; those who lead own the results of those they lead
5. **Whistleblowing vs. cowardice** — the moral and practical analysis of when silence becomes complicity, when speaking up is required despite cost, and how to distinguish genuine moral courage from self-righteous destruction
6. **Moral courage under real risk** — the capacity to act on principle when the social, professional, or personal cost is genuine and the support is absent
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Honor — The Things You Will Not Do

Integrity, at its deepest level, is not about what you do. It is about what you refuse to do.

Everyone has moments when doing the wrong thing would be profitable, easy, or invisible. The question of honor is whether there exists a line you will not cross — a set of actions you will not take regardless of the benefit, regardless of whether anyone would ever know, regardless of the cost of refusal.

An honor code is a personal or communal document that states those lines. Not vaguely — specifically. Not as aspirations — as absolutes. The honor code does not say "I try to be honest." It says "I do not lie, cheat, or steal, and I do not tolerate those who do." The difference between the aspiration and the code is the difference between a preference and a commitment. Preferences bend under pressure. Commitments hold — or they reveal that they were never commitments at all.

**Why honor codes matter:**

Every serious institution that has sustained trust over time has operated from an explicit or implicit honor code. Military academies, religious orders, trade guilds, professional bodies, tribal communities — all have codified the behaviors that disqualify a member. The codes differ in detail but converge on structure: there are things members do, there are things members do not do, and there are things members do not tolerate in others. The three-part structure is universal because it addresses the three failure modes of integrity:

1. **Omission** — failing to do what you should (addressed by "things members do")
2. **Commission** — doing what you should not (addressed by "things members do not do")
3. **Complicity** — tolerating in others what you would not do yourself (addressed by "things members do not tolerate")

The third category is the hardest. It is relatively easy to maintain personal integrity in isolation. It is much harder to maintain it in the face of others' violations — to speak up when someone you know, like, or depend on is acting without integrity. This is where honor codes fail most often, and it is where IC1.3-C spends its most serious attention.

**Building your own honor code:**

You are going to write one. Not because you are told to, but because a person who has not articulated what they will not do has not made the decision — and the person who has not made the decision in advance will almost certainly fail to make it in the moment. The pressure of the moment is too great for real-time moral reasoning. What works in the moment is a pre-made decision: "I do not do that. The decision was made. This situation does not change the decision."

Your honor code should include:
- **What you will do** — the positive commitments of your integrity. "I tell the truth." "I keep my word." "I own my failures."
- **What you will not do** — the lines you refuse to cross. "I do not lie to protect my reputation." "I do not claim credit for work I did not do." "I do not abandon people who depend on me when it becomes inconvenient."
- **What you will not tolerate** — the third category. "I do not stay silent when I witness dishonesty in my group." "I do not allow someone to take blame for something I did." "I do not participate in the destruction of someone's reputation through gossip or false accusation."

This document is living. It will evolve as you grow, as your understanding deepens, and as life presents you with situations you did not anticipate. But it must exist in written form — because a commitment that lives only in your head is a commitment that can be silently revised when the cost of keeping it becomes apparent. Written commitments are harder to abandon. That is the point.

### Part 2: Reputation Capital — Integrity as Strategic Asset

There is a cynical view of integrity that says it is a constraint — something good people do that costs them competitive advantage. This view is wrong, and it is worth understanding why.

Integrity is a long-term strategic asset. It compounds. Every commitment you keep, every truth you tell under cost, every time you own a failure that you could have hidden — each of these actions deposits value into an account that others can see. Over years, this account grows into something economists call reputation capital: the accumulated trust that others have in your reliability, honesty, and character.

Reputation capital has measurable effects:
- **People give you opportunities they would not give others.** The person who is trusted with small things is trusted with large things. The person who is not trusted with small things is not trusted at all.
- **People extend you the benefit of the doubt.** When the trustworthy person makes a mistake, others assume good faith. When the untrustworthy person makes a mistake, others assume the worst. Same action, different interpretation — based entirely on accumulated reputation.
- **People want to work with you, follow you, and partner with you.** In every domain — business, leadership, community, family — the person who can be trusted attracts collaborators. The person who cannot be trusted operates alone, or worse, attracts other untrustworthy people.
- **Your word carries weight.** The person with reputation capital can make commitments that are believed. The person without it can make promises that are doubted. In leadership, in negotiation, in relationships — the ability to be believed is power.

The compounding math is asymmetric. Building reputation capital is slow — it takes consistent, observed behavior over months and years. Losing it is fast — a single act of betrayal, dishonesty, or cowardice at a critical moment can destroy what took years to build. This asymmetry is not punitive. It is structural. Trust is a prediction, and predictions update more on negative evidence than on positive evidence, because negative evidence is more diagnostic. A hundred kept promises tell me you are probably reliable. One broken promise at a critical moment tells me you might not be — and "might not be" is enough to change behavior.

Understanding this asymmetry changes how you approach integrity. You stop seeing honesty as a constraint and start seeing it as an investment. Every act of integrity is a deposit. Every act of dishonesty is a withdrawal that costs more than the deposit was worth. Over decades, the person who understood this math lives a fundamentally different life than the person who did not.

### Part 3: Leadership Accountability — Absorbing Cost Upward

If you are going to lead — in any capacity, in any context — you need to understand a principle that most leaders fail to practice and many never learn: **leadership accountability flows upward.**

When a team succeeds, the credit belongs to the team. When a team fails, the responsibility belongs to the leader. This is not a morale-boosting platitude. It is a structural reality about how accountability works in hierarchies, and it is the single most important distinction between a leader and a person with authority.

**The principle in detail:**

When someone under your leadership fails, there are only a few possibilities:
1. You selected the wrong person for the task. (Your failure of judgment.)
2. You did not train them adequately. (Your failure of preparation.)
3. You did not communicate the standard clearly. (Your failure of communication.)
4. You did not provide the resources they needed. (Your failure of support.)
5. You did not monitor progress and intervene when things went off track. (Your failure of oversight.)
6. The person was fully capable and supported, and simply did not perform. (The one case where the individual bears primary responsibility — but even here, the leader owns the selection decision and the consequence management.)

In every case, the leader's fingerprints are on the failure. Not because the leader literally did everything wrong, but because **leadership means owning the system, not just the tasks.** The leader who says "my team failed" has made a statement about their own leadership. The leader who says "I failed to set my team up for success" has made the same statement — but honestly.

**What this looks like in practice:**

- When your group fails publicly, you do not point at the person who dropped the ball. You say: "We did not deliver. Here is what we are doing about it." Publicly, the accountability is yours. Privately, you address the specific failure with the specific person — directly, honestly, and without the public audience that would make correction feel like humiliation.
- When your team succeeds, you name the specific contributions of specific people. You do not take the credit. Your credit is implicit — you led the team that succeeded. Explicitly claiming it diminishes you and demoralizes the people who did the work.
- When someone on your team makes a mistake that costs the group, you absorb the external consequences — the apology to those affected, the explanation to authority, the plan for repair. Then you address the internal cause with the person responsible, in private, with the same calm accountability you would want applied to you.

This is hard. It means accepting blame for things you did not personally do. It means watching someone else receive credit for work you enabled. It means carrying the weight of outcomes you did not directly produce but are responsible for because you took on the role of leader.

This is why leadership is not a privilege. It is a burden — voluntarily assumed by people who understand what it costs and accept the cost because the mission requires someone to carry it.

### Part 4: Whistleblowing vs. Cowardice — When Silence Becomes Complicity

This is the most difficult section of IC1.3, and it addresses a question that has no clean answer: **when does loyalty to a group become complicity in the group's wrongdoing?**

The easy cases are clear. If you discover that someone in your organization is committing a crime, you report it. If you witness physical abuse, you intervene or call for help. If you see fraud that will harm innocent people, you speak up. These are easy because the moral calculus is unambiguous — the wrong is clear, the victims are identifiable, and silence is obviously participation.

The hard cases are where real moral life happens:

- Your team is cutting corners on a project in ways that are not illegal but are dishonest. The client does not know. Speaking up will slow the project, create conflict, and possibly get you removed from the team. What do you do?
- Your coach is treating a teammate in a way that is harsh and possibly damaging, but is not clearly abusive by any formal definition. Other teammates see it but say nothing. Do you speak up? To whom? At what cost?
- Your friend is cheating — on a test, in a relationship, in a commitment — and has asked you not to say anything. Loyalty to your friend pulls one way. Integrity pulls the other. The person being cheated does not know.
- An authority figure you respect makes a decision that you believe is seriously wrong — not just different from what you would choose, but genuinely harmful. They did not ask for your opinion. Speaking up means challenging someone with power over your situation.

In each of these cases, there is a cost to speaking up and a cost to staying silent. The cost of speaking up is immediate, visible, and personal — social friction, lost relationships, damaged standing, potential retaliation. The cost of staying silent is delayed, invisible, and distributed — the wrong continues, the victims are unprotected, and your own integrity erodes incrementally each day you remain complicit.

**A framework for deciding:**

1. **Is real harm occurring or likely to occur?** Not discomfort — harm. Someone is being damaged, deceived, endangered, or defrauded. If no real harm is occurring, you may be dealing with a disagreement, not a moral crisis. Disagreements are handled differently.
2. **Have you exhausted private channels?** Before going public with a concern, have you raised it directly with the person involved? With a trusted authority? Whistleblowing is the last resort, not the first. The person who skips private correction and goes directly to public exposure is often acting from self-righteousness, not moral courage.
3. **Are you willing to bear the cost?** Genuine moral courage accepts the consequence. If you are only willing to speak up anonymously, with no personal cost, examine your motivation. Anonymous reporting has its place — but it is not moral courage. Moral courage is attaching your name and your reputation to the truth and accepting what follows.
4. **Is your motivation the right one?** Are you speaking up because the situation is wrong, or because you are angry at the person? Because you want to protect the vulnerable, or because you want to punish the powerful? The action might be the same, but the motivation matters for your own integrity. Check it honestly.
5. **What is the cost of continued silence — to others, and to you?** Every day you remain silent in the face of a wrong you could address, your complicity deepens. The question is not just "can I live with the cost of speaking up?" It is also "can I live with the cost of not speaking up?"

**Historical models:**

Study the whistleblowers. Dietrich Bonhoeffer, who conspired against Hitler and was executed for it. Daniel Ellsberg, who released the Pentagon Papers and faced criminal prosecution. Sherron Watkins at Enron, who raised internal alarms that were ignored. Sophie Scholl, who distributed anti-Nazi leaflets as a student and was executed at age 21. In every case, the person paid a real cost — career, freedom, life. In every case, silence would have been easier. In every case, the person decided that the cost of silence was higher than the cost of truth.

You may never face a situation as extreme as these. But you will face situations where the dynamic is the same: you see something wrong, speaking up carries a cost, silence is easier, and your integrity asks you what you are going to do about it. Having studied these people will not make the decision easy. Nothing makes it easy. But it gives you company — the knowledge that others have faced the same question and chosen courage, and that their choice, even when it cost them everything, was not wasted.

### Part 5: Moral Courage Under Real Risk

Moral courage is not the same as bravery. Bravery is the willingness to face physical danger. Moral courage is the willingness to face social, professional, or personal cost for the sake of what is right. Most people who are physically brave are not morally courageous — they will charge a hill under fire but will not tell their boss the project is failing or tell their friend they are being dishonest.

Moral courage is rare because the costs are different. Physical danger produces adrenaline, group solidarity, and often public recognition. Moral courage produces isolation, social punishment, and often public contempt. The person who stands up in a meeting and says "this is wrong" does not receive a medal. They receive silence, followed by consequences.

**What moral courage requires:**

- **A settled identity.** You must know what you believe and what you refuse. If you are still figuring out what you stand for, you will not stand when the pressure comes. This is why IC1.1-C1 (the personal rule of life) is a prerequisite. Your non-negotiables must be decided before the crisis arrives.
- **Tolerance for social pain.** Speaking truth to power — or to peers, which is often harder — produces social friction. People will be angry. People will think you are self-righteous. People will question your motives. You must be able to tolerate this without either collapsing or becoming self-righteous in response.
- **The discipline to act proportionately.** Moral courage is not moral aggression. The person who speaks truth does so clearly, directly, and without unnecessary escalation. They do not enjoy the confrontation. They do not seek it. They accept it because the alternative — silence — is worse.
- **The willingness to be wrong.** Sometimes what you believe is wrong turns out to be more complicated than you thought. Moral courage includes the humility to say "I was wrong" if new information changes the picture. The morally courageous person is not the person who is always right. They are the person who acts on principle and corrects course when they discover their understanding was incomplete.

**What moral courage is not:**

- It is not contrarianism. Disagreeing with everyone does not make you brave. It might make you difficult.
- It is not self-righteousness. The person who speaks up with an air of moral superiority is performing courage, not practicing it. Genuine moral courage is reluctant — the person wishes they did not have to do this.
- It is not seeking martyrdom. The person who goes looking for fights to prove their integrity has confused courage with aggression. Moral courage is responsive to situations, not additive to them.
- It is not ignoring consequences. The morally courageous person considers the cost, accepts it, and acts. The person who acts without considering the cost is not brave — they are impulsive.

### Part 6: The Honor Code as Living Document

In Part 1, you began writing your honor code. Now you will complete it — not as a finished document, but as a living one that you will revise as your understanding deepens and your experience expands.

Your honor code integrates everything in IC1.3:
- The truth-telling habits of Stretching Branches — now operating as non-negotiable reflexes
- The commitment-keeping and trust mechanics of Weathering Storms — now operating as settled practices
- The honor lines of Standing Ground — the things you will not do, the standards you will not lower, the wrongs you will not tolerate

**The structure:**

1. **Statement of identity.** Who you are. Not your resume — your character. What you stand for, what you are building, what you serve.
2. **Positive commitments.** What you will always do. Specific, observable, testable. Not "be honest" — "Tell the truth, including when it costs me, including when I could get away with the lie."
3. **Lines you will not cross.** What you will never do. Specific, non-negotiable, unconditional. Not "try to avoid dishonesty" — "I will not lie to protect my reputation, ever."
4. **What you will not tolerate.** What you will not stay silent about when you see it in others. This is the hardest section because it commits you to action that carries social cost.
5. **Accountability structure.** How you will hold yourself to this code. Who will hold you to it. How often you will review it. What happens when you violate it — because you will, eventually, and the code must address that too.

This document is private. It is not for display. It is not a performance of virtue for an audience. It is between you and yourself — and, if you choose, between you and a mentor or a small group of people who hold you to it. Its power comes from its honesty and its specificity, not from its visibility.

Review it quarterly. Revise it as you grow. Test it against your actual behavior — not against your aspirations. The honor code that describes who you actually are under pressure is valuable. The honor code that describes who you wish you were is fiction.
content.formative_practices:
### Practice 1: Case Study Analysis [Biweekly]
Every two weeks, study a case of moral courage — historical, contemporary, fictional. Analyze: What was the situation? What did integrity require? What was the cost? What did the person do? Was it the right call? Write a 500-word analysis. Discuss with your mentor. The goal is to practice moral reasoning before you need it in real time.

### Practice 2: Honor Code Writing and Revision [Initial Draft + Quarterly Review]
Write your personal honor code using the structure in Part 6. Review it quarterly against your actual behavior. Where did you live up to it? Where did you fall short? What needs revision — the code, or the behavior? Discuss with your mentor.

### Practice 3: Commitment Tracking — Extended [Ongoing]
Continue the commitment log from Weathering Storms, but expand its scope: track not just whether you kept your word, but whether you spoke up when you should have, stayed silent when you should not have, and owned outcomes you were responsible for. This is a broader accountability measure that covers the Standing Ground territory of honor and moral courage.

### Practice 4: Scenario Analysis [Monthly]
Once a month, work through a scenario with your mentor — a situation where integrity is tested, where the right answer is not obvious, and where the cost of the right action is real. These are not trick questions. They are genuine dilemmas. Write your reasoning. Identify the competing values. Decide what you would do. Defend the decision.

### Practice 5: Leadership Accountability Practice [As Opportunities Arise]
When you are in a leadership role — any role where others depend on your decisions — practice the leadership accountability framework: own the results publicly, address the causes privately, distribute credit to those who did the work. Track these instances in your journal. Note what was hard about it. Note what you learned.

### Practice 6: The Integrity Audit [Biannual]
Twice a year, conduct a thorough integrity audit. Review your honor code against your actual behavior over the past six months. Identify the gaps. Be specific: not "I could do better" but "On [date], in [situation], I failed to [specific action] and instead [specific alternative]. This violates [specific line in my honor code]." Then: what is the repair? What changes?

mentor_guide.formation_environment:
### Creating Space for Genuine Moral Reasoning

The Standing Ground student needs to reason through moral dilemmas without being given the answer. This is harder for the mentor than it sounds, because the temptation is to guide the student toward the "right" conclusion. But many IC1.3-C scenarios have no single right answer — they have tradeoffs, costs, competing principles, and uncertain outcomes. The mentor's role is to help the student think clearly, not to think for them.

**Practical guidelines:**

- **Present case studies without telegraphing the correct answer.** If you present the Bonhoeffer case and your tone makes clear that resistance was obviously the right choice, you have eliminated the moral reasoning. Present it neutrally: "Here is what happened. Here are the options he faced. Here are the costs of each. What would you do?" Let the student struggle.
- **Push back on easy answers.** If the student says "obviously you speak up," ask: "At what cost? To whom? What if you are wrong? What if speaking up makes things worse?" If the student says "obviously you stay quiet," ask: "For how long? At what point does silence become participation? Who is being harmed by your silence?"
- **Tolerate ambiguity.** Some conversations should end without resolution. "I do not know what I would do in that situation" is an honest answer and, for a teenager, a sign of genuine moral development. The person who always knows the right answer has not yet understood the question.
- **Model moral humility.** Share your own uncertainty. "I have thought about situations like this and I am not sure what I would do." This is not weakness — it is honesty about the difficulty of moral life. The student who sees their mentor grapple honestly with difficult questions learns that moral reasoning is serious work, not a performance of certainty.

### Supporting the Honor Code Process

The personal honor code is the central written artifact of IC1.3-C. It must be the student's own work — not dictated by you, not borrowed from a source, not performed for approval. Your role is to help the student make it honest and specific, and to test it against their actual behavior.

**Guidance for the process:**

- **Insist on specificity.** "I will be honest" is not specific enough. "I will tell the truth even when it costs me" is better. "I will not lie to protect my reputation, and when I discover I have misrepresented something, I will correct it immediately regardless of the social cost" is the level of specificity that makes an honor code operational.
- **Challenge aspirational language.** If the code describes who the student wishes they were rather than who they actually are under pressure, name that gap: "This is a good aspiration. Is it how you actually behave? When was the last time this line was tested?" The code must be honest about where the student actually stands, not where they want to be.
- **Treat the code as confidential.** The student may share it with you, and that sharing is a trust investment. Do not share it with others without permission. Do not use it as a weapon: "Your honor code says X, but you did Y!" Use it as a conversation tool: "Let us look at your code and talk about how the week went in light of what you wrote."
- **Expect revision.** The first draft will be incomplete. Life will test lines the student did not anticipate. Revision is a sign of growth, not failure. A code that never changes is a code that is not being tested.

### Navigating the Whistleblowing Question

This is the most sensitive topic in IC1.3-C, and it requires particular care because:

1. The student may have real-life situations that fall into this territory. They may be aware of wrongdoing by a peer, a coach, a teacher, or another adult. Your discussion of the topic must be grounded enough that the student can apply it to real situations, not just historical ones.
2. The line between "speaking up" and "tattling" is significant to this age group. The student needs to understand that there is a real and important difference between reporting genuine harm and reporting minor violations out of self-interest or social competition. Help them develop the judgment to distinguish between the two.
3. Some whistleblowing situations may involve you. If the student develops the moral courage IC1.3-C is designed to produce, they may one day tell you that something you are doing is wrong. Your response in that moment is the ultimate test of your own IC1.3 formation. Receive it. Consider it. If they are right, say so. If they are wrong, explain why — but acknowledge the courage it took to raise the issue.

**Framework for the student:**

- Private channels first. Always. The person who skips private correction and goes public is often acting from a motive other than integrity.
- Proportionality. The response should match the severity of the wrong. Not every problem requires a dramatic confrontation.
- Willingness to be wrong. The student should understand that they might be misreading the situation. Moral courage includes the humility to correct course.
- Acceptance of cost. If the student is only willing to speak up when there is no risk, they are not practicing moral courage. They are practicing convenience.

### Your Own Integrity at Standing Ground

This section is for you, not the student.

The Standing Ground student is studying you with adult-level perception. They see when you cut corners. They notice when your words and actions diverge. They register when you demand accountability from them but excuse it in yourself. They observe how you handle your own failures, your own moments of moral testing, your own encounters with authority.

You do not need to be perfect. You need to be honest. The mentor who says "I faced a situation like that last year, and I did not handle it well, and here is what I learned" is doing more IC1.3-C formation than the mentor who presents a flawless facade. The student at this age does not need a perfect model. They need an honest one — a person who is genuinely trying to live with integrity, who fails sometimes, and who handles the failures the way IC1.3 describes: with ownership, repair, and genuine change.

If there are areas where your own integrity is weak — and everyone has them — consider whether IC1.3-C is asking you to address them. Not for the student's sake alone, though that matters. For your own. The framework forms the mentor as well as the student.
mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Personal Honor Code

**Developing well:** The student has written a specific, honest, operationally useful honor code. It describes real behavioral commitments, not vague aspirations. It has been tested against actual experience and revised based on what the student learned. The student can discuss it thoughtfully — what lines have been tested, where they held, where they did not.

**Developing slowly:** The code is vague, aspirational, or borrowed rather than personal. It reads like a list of virtues rather than a set of behavioral commitments. The student has not tested it against actual behavior or revised it based on experience. It is a document on paper, not a standard in practice.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student had real-life situations that tested the code? If not, the code has not yet been forged in experience. Create or identify situations where the student's commitments are genuinely tested — not artificial scenarios, but real contexts with real stakes. Also: is the student honest about gaps? A student who claims the code has never been violated is either extraordinarily disciplined or not paying close enough attention. The second is more likely.

### 2. Moral Courage in Practice

**Developing well:** The student has demonstrated, in real situations, the willingness to speak up when something is wrong — to a peer, to a group, to an authority figure. They did so despite social cost. They were proportionate and direct, not dramatic or self-righteous. They accepted the consequences.

**Developing slowly:** The student articulates moral courage well in discussion but does not practice it in real life. When confronted with actual situations requiring courage, they rationalize silence: "It was not that bad." "Someone else will handle it." "It is not my business." The gap between articulation and action is the gap IC1.3-C must close.

**Critical note:** Some students will not have faced a genuine moral courage situation yet. That is acceptable — you cannot manufacture the crisis. What you can assess is readiness: Does the student understand what moral courage requires? Can they analyze case studies with nuance? Do they demonstrate the disposition — the willingness to accept social cost for principle — in smaller situations? A student who speaks an unpopular truth in a group discussion, who tells a friend they are being dishonest, or who raises a concern with you about something they disagree with is demonstrating the capacity even before the big test arrives.

### 3. Leadership Accountability

**Developing well:** In any leadership context — formal or informal — the student absorbs accountability for group outcomes. They do not blame subordinates publicly. They distribute credit to those who did the work. They address failures privately and own them publicly. This is observable in how they talk about group results: "we" for failures, specific names for successes.

**Developing slowly:** The student takes credit for successes and distributes blame for failures. In discussion, they understand the principle. In practice, they revert to self-protective instincts. The gap between understanding and practice is where the work is.

**Diagnostic questions:** Does the student have genuine leadership experience — not title, but actual responsibility for others' outcomes? If not, the competence marker cannot be meaningfully assessed. Create or identify contexts where the student leads a small group through a real task with real consequences. Observe how they handle both success and failure.

### 4. Whistleblowing and Complicity Analysis

**Developing well:** The student can analyze situations where loyalty and integrity conflict with genuine nuance. They do not default to "always speak up" (which ignores cost and context) or "always stay loyal" (which enables complicity). They consider the severity of the wrong, the availability of private channels, the likelihood that speaking up will actually address the problem, and the cost to themselves and others. They have internalized the framework and can apply it to novel situations.

**Developing slowly:** The student defaults to one pole — either "always report everything" (rigid, lacking judgment) or "loyalty first, always" (avoidant, enabling). They cannot analyze the gray area between clear wrongdoing and honest disagreement. They have not internalized the framework well enough to apply it to situations they have not studied.

### 5. Reputation Capital Understanding

**Developing well:** The student demonstrates, through behavior over months and years, that they understand integrity as a long-term investment. Their reliability is consistent across contexts — not just when stakes are high or when they are observed. They can articulate the compounding math of trust — how small, consistent deposits of integrity produce disproportionate long-term returns — and their behavior reflects this understanding.

**Developing slowly:** The student understands the concept intellectually but their behavior is context-dependent. They are reliable when visibility is high and less reliable when it is low. They treat integrity as a cost to be minimized rather than an investment to be maximized. They have not yet internalized the long-term strategic value of trustworthiness.

### 6. Integrity Audit Practice

**Developing well:** The student conducts biannual integrity audits that are honest, specific, and action-oriented. They identify real gaps between their honor code and their behavior, name specific incidents, and develop concrete plans for change. The audits show genuine self-knowledge and a willingness to confront uncomfortable truths about themselves.

**Developing slowly:** The audits are vague, self-congratulatory, or performative. The student identifies no significant gaps (suggesting dishonesty with themselves) or identifies gaps without developing repair plans (suggesting the audit is a box-checking exercise rather than genuine self-examination).
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Producing Moralists Instead of Moral People
The greatest risk of IC1.3-C is producing a student who can articulate moral principles beautifully and does not live them. Moral articulation is not moral character. A student who writes an excellent honor code, analyzes case studies brilliantly, and delivers compelling narration responses may still be dishonest, unreliable, or cowardly in actual moral moments. The assessment must be behavioral, not verbal. What does the student do under pressure, when you are not watching, when it costs them something? That is the data that matters.

### Encouraging Self-Righteousness
The student who has studied moral courage may develop a sense of moral superiority — "I see the truth that others are too cowardly to speak." This is not moral courage. It is self-righteousness, and it is one of the most corrosive dispositions a person can develop. Moral courage is reluctant. The person of genuine integrity wishes the situation did not require them to speak up. They do not enjoy the confrontation. They do not seek moral crises to prove their courage. If you see the student developing a pattern of seeking moral confrontation, or of consistently positioning themselves as the righteous truth-teller in a world of cowards, intervene. This is a character flaw dressed as a virtue.

### Making the Honor Code Performative
The honor code must remain private and honest. If it becomes a document for display — shared on social media, presented to peers, used as social signaling — it has been corrupted. An honor code that is written for an audience is a brand statement, not a moral commitment. Keep it between the student, the mentor, and a very small circle of accountability partners. If the student begins performing their honor code, redirect: "This is between you and your character. It is not a public document. It is a private standard."

### Oversimplifying Whistleblowing
The whistleblowing section is the most dangerous to teach poorly. If you reduce it to "always speak up, no matter what," you are training the student to be reckless and self-righteous. If you reduce it to "be careful, think it through, consider the costs," you may be training them to rationalize silence. The reality is that the right answer depends on the specific situation, and developing the judgment to navigate that specificity is the work. Do not give them a formula. Give them a framework and help them develop the judgment to use it wisely.

### Ignoring the Student's Real Moral Situations
Case studies are valuable. But the student's actual life is the real formation ground. If the student is dealing with a genuine moral dilemma — a friend who is cheating, a group that is cutting corners, an authority figure who is behaving badly — that real situation is more important than any historical case study. Create space for the student to bring real moral questions to the discussion. Handle them with the seriousness they deserve. And recognize that your advice in those moments may be the most consequential mentoring you do.

### Demanding Courage You Would Not Practice Yourself
Before you push the student toward moral courage, ask yourself honestly: would you do what you are asking them to do? If you would stay silent in the same situation, your instruction is hollow, and the student will sense it. Moral courage is modeled before it is taught. If you are asking the student to develop a capacity you have not developed yourself, start developing it. IC1.3-C forms the mentor as surely as it forms the student.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Honor Code
**Strong:** The student can articulate their code's specific commitments, describe situations that tested them, and honestly assess where they held and where they fell short. The conversation reveals genuine self-knowledge and an ongoing relationship with the document — it is alive, not static.
**Weak:** The student recites principles without connecting them to behavior. The code has not been tested, or the student cannot identify any gap between the code and their actual conduct. The code is aspirational fiction rather than operational standard.

### Prompt 2: Integrity Requiring Courage
**Strong:** Specific situation — ideally from the student's own life, though historical examples are acceptable if the analysis is personal. The student describes the cost clearly, wrestles with the decision honestly, and arrives at a reasoned position without false certainty. If they describe their own experience, they are honest about what was hard and what they learned.
**Weak:** Abstract principles without concrete application. "You should always do the right thing." No engagement with the cost. No personal connection to the difficulty.

### Prompt 3: Leadership Accountability
**Strong:** Clear understanding of the principle with specific examples — ideally from the student's own leadership experience. Can distinguish between leadership accountability (owning the system) and individual accountability (owning your actions). Can identify real leaders who practiced it and real leaders who did not, with specific behavioral evidence.
**Weak:** Can state the principle but cannot apply it. Leadership accountability is understood as "take the blame" without the deeper understanding of why and how. No personal experience to draw on.

### Prompt 4: Loyalty vs. Complicity
**Strong:** Nuanced reasoning. The student can identify the line — or more accurately, the range — where loyalty becomes complicity. They reference the framework: severity of harm, availability of private channels, willingness to bear cost, honest examination of motive. They acknowledge that the line is not always clear and that good people can disagree about where it falls.
**Weak:** Binary thinking. "You should always report." "You should always be loyal." No ability to navigate the gray area. No acknowledgment of the difficulty.

### Prompt 5: Reputation Capital
**Strong:** Honest self-assessment grounded in specific behavioral evidence. The student can identify what their reputation is based on specific things they have done (and not done). They can identify investments they have made and costs they have incurred. They can name what the people who know them best would say — and the answer includes both strengths and gaps.
**Weak:** Self-assessment disconnected from evidence. "People see me as honest" without being able to cite specific instances that would support (or undermine) that claim. Or unwillingness to engage in honest self-assessment at all.

### Prompt 6: Failing Your Own Standard
**Strong:** The student names a specific instance where they violated their own honor code or failed to live up to their stated values. They describe what happened without excusing it, identify what they learned, and describe what they changed. There is no self-pity and no self-flagellation — only honest accounting and forward motion.
**Weak:** Cannot identify an instance (suggesting either dishonesty or insufficient self-examination). Or the instance is described with extensive context designed to minimize the failure. Or the response is performatively guilty — "I am such a terrible person" — which substitutes emotional display for genuine accountability.
